items.text: How do specific gut microbiome bacteria species influence host immune regulation and inflammatory responses in the colon?
Which dietary fiber types most effectively increase alpha diversity of the gut microbiome and promote beneficial bacteria growth?
What is the causal relationship between butyrate-producing bacteria and regulatory T-cell expansion in the gut?
Short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), particularly butyrate produced by gut bacteria, are key mediators of the gut-immune axis.
High dietary fiber intake promotes microbiome diversity and colonization by Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus species.
Germ-free mice colonized with Clostridiales bacteria showed significantly elevated Foxp3+ regulatory T cells, implicating gut microbiota in immune tolerance.
Prebiotic supplementation increased stool butyrate concentration and correlated with reduced intestinal permeability markers in a 12-week cohort study.
16S rRNA sequencing of stool samples from dietary fiber intervention trial (n=80) to compare microbiome composition changes after 8 weeks.
